executiveSummary:
**Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis**

National Medicaid enrollment has reached 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with nearly all states experiencing enrollment declines following the end of the public health emergency continuous coverage provisions. The most significant decreases occurred in Michigan (-7.6%) and California and Illinois (both -6.2%), while North Carolina bucked the trend with modest growth (+0.5%). Total national spending for fiscal year 2024 reached $215.9 billion with a per-enrollee average of $2,869, though significant variation exists across states. Pennsylvania leads in per-enrollee costs at $4,377, followed by Massachusetts at $4,201, while Florida maintains the lowest costs at $2,031 per enrollee. The federal government's share represents approximately 66% of total program expenditures at $142.0 billion.

Texas, with 4.03 million enrollees, experienced a moderate 3.1% enrollment decline, positioning it similarly to Pennsylvania in terms of year-over-year changes. The state's per-enrollee spending of $2,988 falls just above the national average, ranking fourth nationally in total expenditures at $12.0 billion despite serving fewer beneficiaries than New York. Texas demonstrates strong managed care penetration at 93.3%, well above the national average of 83.2%, with nearly 3.9 million enrollees in managed care arrangements. However, quality performance presents opportunities for improvement, with Texas scoring 62 out of 100 and ranking 30th nationally, trailing high-performing states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80) by significant margins. Recent regulatory developments signal increased federal focus on home and community-based services quality measures and interoperability standards that will require state attention and potential program modifications in the coming fiscal periods.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing 5.4% of total national enrollment despite serving the second-most populous state. The program's per-enrollee spending of $2,988 sits above the national average of $2,869 but remains significantly below peer large states like New York ($3,431) and Pennsylvania ($4,377). Compared to southern non-expansion peers, Texas spends considerably more per enrollee than Florida ($2,031) and North Carolina ($2,616), while its -3.1% year-over-year enrollment decline aligns with the national trend of post-pandemic unwinding, performing better than large states like California and Illinois (both -6.2%) but lagging behind North Carolina's modest growth of 0.5%.

Texas demonstrates operational strengths in managed care adoption with 93.3% penetration—well above the 83.2% national average—indicating effective care coordination infrastructure across its geographically diverse population. However, quality outcomes present a significant challenge, with the state ranking 30th nationally at 62 points, placing it in the moderate tier but trailing the national average of 65.6 and performing poorly compared to leading states. This quality gap is particularly concerning given Texas's higher per-enrollee spending relative to some peers, suggesting efficiency challenges that warrant monitoring. The state's position among non-expansion states creates ongoing tension between managing costs and improving access, while upcoming federal HCBS quality measures and interoperability requirements signal areas where Texas will need sustained investment to improve its competitive position among large state programs.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: Five States Show Critically Low Quality Performance
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored below 55 on quality measures, well below the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Florida Shows Low Per-Enrollee Spending Pattern
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average, potentially indicating underinvestment in beneficiary care.

alerts.title: Texas: High Managed Care But Moderate Quality Scores
alerts.description: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration (above 83.2% national average) but quality scores of only 62, suggesting efficiency gains aren't translating to quality.
alerts.sourceLabel: CMS Quality and Managed Care Data

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score at risk - ranks #30 with only 62/100 performance
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scored 62/100 on quality measures, ranking 30th nationally and falling in the 'moderate' tier. This is significantly below the national average of 65.6 and well short of high-performer threshold of 75.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Poor quality scores can trigger federal oversight, impact funding, and create reputational risks for HHSC. Accenture's contract value depends on demonstrating measurable improvements.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs 4% above national average at $2,988
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, representing $119 excess per member. With 4M+ enrollees, this suggests significant cost optimization opportunity.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Cost efficiency is a key performance metric for Medicaid contracts and represents potential savings of $480M+ if brought to national average levels.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost drivers and benchmark against efficient states like Florida ($2,031)

executiveInsights.title: New federal HCBS quality measures coming 2028 - regulatory compliance gap
executiveInsights.summary: CMS is implementing new Home and Community-Based Services quality measures for 2028. Texas's current quality performance suggests potential compliance challenges.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New regulatory requirements will impact contract deliverables and create additional compliance obligations that Accenture must prepare HHSC to meet.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review 2028 HCBS quality measure requirements and assess Texas readiness

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declining 3.1% YoY - 126K fewer members than expected
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% year-over-year, slightly worse than the national decline trend. This represents approximately 126,000 fewer enrollees than prior year levels.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines can impact contract economics and may signal operational issues in eligibility processing or member retention that require immediate attention.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag enrollment trends to HHSC and analyze root causes of decline

executiveInsights.title: HHS OIG active Medicaid oversight creates audit risk exposure
executiveInsights.summary: HHS OIG has active Medicaid portfolio oversight and in-progress work plan items that could impact Texas operations. Multiple regulatory changes are also in motion affecting interoperability and prior authorization.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Active federal oversight increases audit risk and regulatory compliance requirements that could affect contract performance and create additional deliverable obligations.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review current OIG work plan items for Texas-specific risks

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality mandate
riskOpportunity.description: CMS mandating new Home and Community-Based Services quality measures by 2028 requiring significant system and process overhauls.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position as the lead integration partner for HCBS quality measurement system implementation.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline impact
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY, potentially reducing contract values and operational scope.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop efficiency-focused service delivery models to maintain margins despite lower enrollment.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality score gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating pressure for improvement initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose comprehensive quality improvement program targeting top-tier performance metrics.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: High 93.3% managed care penetration suggests potential large-scale contract renewals or competitive procurements.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish dedicated team to monitor and prepare for upcoming managed care organization contract cycles.

riskOpportunity.label: Interoperability compliance
riskOpportunity.description: New federal interoperability and prior authorization standards requiring technology infrastructure upgrades.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop standardized interoperability solution offering for rapid deployment across Texas MCOs.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG audit exposure
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG oversight of Medicaid programs increases compliance risk and audit preparation needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring capabilities and offer proactive audit readiness services to clients.

riskOpportunity.label: Cost efficiency pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas per-enrollee spending of $2,988 above national average creates pressure for cost reduction initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop data-driven cost optimization strategies targeting administrative and clinical efficiency gains.

riskOpportunity.label: Procurement pipeline expansion
riskOpportunity.description: Active Texas HHSC procurement portal indicates ongoing opportunities for new contract vehicles and service areas.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish systematic monitoring and rapid response capability for emerging procurement opportunities.
